county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Lee County formed 1866 from Itawamba and Pontotoc counties. County seat is Tupelo, birthplace of Elvis Presley. The Battle of Tupelo (1864) and Brice's Cross Roads (1864) were fought nearby. Named for Robert E. Lee.
county_metadata.research_tips: Lee County (formed 1866 from Itawamba and Pontotoc) has Tupelo as county seat, birthplace of Elvis Presley. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books and chancery court records from 1867. For pre-1866 records, check Itawamba County (Fulton) and Pontotoc County (Pontotoc). MDAH has microfilm of Lee County records. The Tupelo-Lee County Library has a Mississippi Room with local history collections. The Battle of Tupelo (1864) left military records at NARA. BLM GLO land patents document Chickasaw Cession entries. Freedmen's Bureau records for northeast Mississippi cover the area. The Natchez Trace Parkway headquarters is near Tupelo. FamilySearch has digitized Lee County clerk records. The county grew as a railroad center in the late 1800s, and railroad employment records may be useful.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Lee County deed books from 1866; Chancery Clerk in Tupelo maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Leflore County formed 1871 from Carroll, Sunflower, and Tallahatchie counties. County seat is Greenwood. Named for Greenwood Leflore, mixed-race Choctaw chief and Mississippi state senator. A Delta county with extensive cotton plantations.
county_metadata.research_tips: Leflore County (formed 1871 from Carroll, Sunflower, and Tallahatchie) has Greenwood as county seat. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books and chancery court records from 1871. For pre-1871 records, check the parent counties. Leflore County is a Delta county named for Greenwood Leflore, the last Choctaw chief to sign a removal treaty. MDAH has microfilm of Leflore County records. The county had enormous cotton plantations with a predominantly African American population. Plantation records and slave schedules at MDAH are essential for pre-war research. Freedmen's Bureau records for the Greenwood sub-district are extensive. The 1955 Emmett Till murder trial in Sumner (Tallahatchie County) also involved Leflore County. FamilySearch has digitized Leflore County clerk records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Leflore County deed books from 1871; Chancery Clerk in Greenwood maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Lincoln County formed 1870 from Copiah, Franklin, Lawrence, and Pike counties. County seat is Brookhaven. Located in southwest Mississippi. Named for General Benjamin Lincoln of the Revolutionary War.
county_metadata.research_tips: Lincoln County (formed 1870 from Copiah, Franklin, Lawrence, and Pike) has Brookhaven as county seat. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books and chancery court records from 1870. For pre-1870 records, check the four parent counties. MDAH has microfilm of Lincoln County records. Lincoln County is in southwest Mississippi with a mixed economy of farming, timber, and dairy. BLM GLO land patents document earlier federal entries in the area. Freedmen's Bureau records for southwest Mississippi cover the county. The Lincoln County Historical and Genealogical Society maintains indexes and publications. FamilySearch has digitized Lincoln County clerk records. Baptist and Methodist church records from the 1840s-1870s document families before and after county formation.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Lincoln County deed books from 1870; Chancery Clerk in Brookhaven maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Lowndes County formed 1830 from Monroe County. County seat is Columbus, which has the largest collection of antebellum homes in Mississippi. Columbus Air Force Base is a major installation. Named for a congressman from South Carolina.
county_metadata.research_tips: Lowndes County (formed 1830 from Monroe) has Columbus as county seat. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books from 1830 and chancery court records. Columbus was a major antebellum center and escaped Civil War destruction, preserving many records. MDAH has extensive microfilm of Lowndes County records. The Columbus-Lowndes Public Library has a local history collection. Columbus has the largest collection of antebellum homes in Mississippi, and associated family and plantation records are at MDAH. The county had a very large enslaved population. Freedmen's Bureau records for the Columbus sub-district are essential. Columbus Air Force Base records may be useful for 20th-century families. FamilySearch has digitized Lowndes County clerk records. Church records from First United Methodist (1824) and other early congregations document founding families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Lowndes County deed books from 1830; Chancery Clerk in Columbus maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Madison County formed 1828 from Yazoo County. County seat is Canton. Part of the Jackson metropolitan area. Canton was significant in the civil rights movement. Named for President James Madison.
county_metadata.research_tips: Madison County (formed 1828 from Yazoo) has Canton as county seat. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books from 1828 and chancery court records. MDAH has microfilm of Madison County land records, marriage books, and chancery minutes. Madison County borders Hinds County and is part of the Jackson metropolitan area. The county had a large enslaved population on cotton plantations. Estate inventories and slave schedules at MDAH are important. Freedmen's Bureau records for the Jackson sub-district cover Madison County. Canton was significant in the civil rights movement. BLM GLO land patents document Choctaw Cession entries. FamilySearch has digitized Madison County clerk records. Baptist and Methodist church records from the 1830s document early families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Madison County deed books from 1828; Chancery Clerk in Canton maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.record_losses: 1881 courthouse fire destroyed many early records
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Marion County formed 1811 from Amite, Franklin, and Wayne counties. County seat is Columbia. Burned county: 1881 courthouse fire destroyed many early records. Located in south Mississippi. Named for General Francis Marion, the 'Swamp Fox.'
county_metadata.research_tips: Marion County (formed 1811 from Amite, Franklin, and Wayne) is a burned county due to the 1881 courthouse fire. County seat is Columbia. The Chancery Clerk holds surviving and reconstructed records. For the burned period, use BLM GLO federal land patents (glorecords.blm.gov), parent county records, and MDAH microfilm. Marion County is in the Piney Woods with a timber and farming economy. Columbia was the original home of Marion Military Institute (now in Alabama). Freedmen's Bureau records cover the county. Church records from Baptist congregations are important supplements to the burned civil records. FamilySearch has some Marion County records. Tax rolls at MDAH partially fill gaps. Census records from 1820 forward are available through NARA.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Marion County deed books from 1811; Chancery Clerk in Columbia maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.record_losses: 1863 and 1864 Civil War raids by Union forces damaged or destroyed records; multiple courthouse incidents
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Marshall County formed 1836 from the Chickasaw Cession of 1832. County seat is Holly Springs. Burned county: 1863-1864 Civil War raids damaged or destroyed records. Holly Springs was a prosperous antebellum town raided repeatedly during the war. Named for Chief Justice John Marshall.
county_metadata.research_tips: Marshall County (formed 1836 from the Chickasaw Cession of 1832) is a burned county due to 1863 and 1864 Civil War raids by Union forces. County seat is Holly Springs. The Chancery Clerk holds reconstructed and post-war records. Holly Springs was raided repeatedly during the war, with Confederate General Van Dorn's raid in 1862 and subsequent Union retaliation. For the burned period, use BLM GLO land patents (glorecords.blm.gov), MDAH microfilm, and census records. The Marshall County Historical Museum in Holly Springs holds local collections. MDAH has records of some surviving pre-war materials. Holly Springs Female Institute records and plantation records are at MDAH. Freedmen's Bureau records for the Holly Springs sub-district are extensive. FamilySearch has digitized available Marshall County records. Rust College (1866, HBCU) records document African American families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Marshall County deed books from 1836; Chancery Clerk in Holly Springs maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Monroe County formed 1821 from the Chickasaw Cession of 1816. County seat is Aberdeen, on the Tombigbee River. A prosperous antebellum cotton county. Named for President James Monroe.
county_metadata.research_tips: Monroe County (formed 1821 from the Chickasaw Cession of 1816) has Aberdeen as county seat. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books from 1821 and chancery court records. Monroe County has well-preserved early records. MDAH has extensive microfilm of Monroe County land records, chancery minutes, and marriage books. Aberdeen was a prosperous antebellum town on the Tombigbee River. The county had a large enslaved population, and plantation records are at MDAH. BLM GLO land patents document Chickasaw Cession entries. The Aberdeen Tri-Weekly Examiner and other local newspapers are at MDAH. Freedmen's Bureau records for the Aberdeen sub-district cover Monroe County. FamilySearch has digitized Monroe County clerk records. Church records from First Presbyterian Aberdeen (1837) and other early congregations are important sources.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Monroe County deed books from 1821; Chancery Clerk in Aberdeen maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

source_profiles.source_name: Monroe County Chancery Court Records (Probate) -- Chancery Clerk / MDAH
source_profiles.repository: Monroe County Chancery Clerk, False / Mississippi Department of Archives and History, Jackson
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Mississippi uses Chancery Court for probate (unique system -- no separate probate court). Wills, estates, guardianships, and land partitions for Monroe County from 1821; MDAH has microfilm of chancery minutes and estate files
source_profiles.survival_notes: Generally well-preserved from 1821

source_profiles.source_name: Monroe County Marriage Records -- Chancery Clerk / FamilySearch
source_profiles.repository: Monroe County Chancery Clerk, False / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records for Monroe County from 1821; FamilySearch has indexed many Mississippi county marriage records; state marriage registration began 1926 through Mississippi State Department of Health

source_profiles.source_name: Monroe County Federal Census Records
source_profiles.repository: NARA / FamilySearch / Ancestry
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census for Monroe County from 1830 through 1950; includes slave schedules 1850-1860 listing enslaved persons by owner; agricultural and manufacturing schedules provide economic data; the 1890 census was destroyed nationally

source_profiles.source_name: Monroe County Chickasaw Cession and Removal Records -- NARA / BLM GLO
source_profiles.repository: NARA (Record Group 75) / Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Monroe County was formed from Chickasaw Cession lands (Treaty of Pontotoc, 1832). BLM GLO records document federal land sales following removal. NARA Record Group 75 holds Chickasaw removal rolls, allotment records, and agency correspondence

source_profiles.source_name: Monroe County Military Records -- MDAH / NARA / Fold3
source_profiles.repository: Mississippi Department of Archives and History, Jackson / NARA / Fold3
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Civil War Confederate service records for Monroe County soldiers; Mississippi contributed heavily to the Confederate army; MDAH holds state adjutant general records; NARA has compiled Confederate service records; Fold3 has digitized many; also includes WWI/WWII draft registrations

source_profiles.source_name: Monroe County Cemetery Records -- Find A Grave / MSGenWeb
source_profiles.repository: Find A Grave / MSGenWeb / Local historical societies
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Cemetery transcriptions for Monroe County churches, community cemeteries, and family burial grounds; Find A Grave has growing coverage; MSGenWeb volunteers have transcribed many Monroe County cemeteries; particularly valuable when civil records are incomplete or destroyed

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Montgomery County formed 1871 from Carroll and Choctaw counties. County seat is Winona. Located in north-central Mississippi in the hills east of the Delta. Named for General Richard Montgomery of the Revolutionary War.
county_metadata.research_tips: Montgomery County (formed 1871 from Carroll and Choctaw) has Winona as county seat. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books and chancery court records from 1871. For pre-1871 records, check Carroll County (Carrollton) and Choctaw County (Ackerman -- note 1881 fire). MDAH has microfilm of Montgomery County records. BLM GLO land patents document Choctaw Cession entries in the area. Montgomery County is in the hills east of the Delta with a cotton and timber economy. Freedmen's Bureau records cover the county. FamilySearch has some Montgomery County records. Church records from Baptist and Methodist congregations supplement civil records. Tax rolls at MDAH trace landholding families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Montgomery County deed books from 1871; Chancery Clerk in Winona maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

source_profiles.source_name: Montgomery County Deed Books -- Chancery Clerk / MDAH
source_profiles.repository: Montgomery County Chancery Clerk, False / Mississippi Department of Archives and History, Jackson
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books for Montgomery County from 1871; MDAH holds microfilm copies; Chancery Clerk in False maintains originals; Mississippi is a public land state with BLM GLO patents available online
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete from 1871; original deed books held by Chancery Clerk in False

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Neshoba County formed 1833 from the Choctaw Cession of 1830. County seat is Philadelphia. Home to the Mississippi Band of Choctaw Indians tribal headquarters. Site of the 1964 civil rights murders of Chaney, Goodman, and Schwerner. The Neshoba County Fair is a major annual cultural event.
county_metadata.research_tips: Neshoba County (formed 1833 from Choctaw Cession) has Philadelphia as county seat. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books from 1834 and chancery court records. MDAH has microfilm of Neshoba County records. Neshoba County is home to the Mississippi Band of Choctaw Indians tribal headquarters and reservation lands. Choctaw tribal records, allotment records, and census rolls are at NARA (Record Group 75) and the Choctaw tribal archives. The annual Neshoba County Fair is a major cultural event. BLM GLO land patents document Choctaw Cession entries. The county was the site of the 1964 civil rights murders of Chaney, Goodman, and Schwerner -- FBI records and trial documents are available. FamilySearch has digitized Neshoba County clerk records. Baptist church records from the 1830s are important.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Neshoba County deed books from 1833; Chancery Clerk in Philadelphia maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov
